docs.title: go-crap
docs.text:
go-crap is a CLI tool that calculates the CRAP score (cyclomatic complexity x coverage) for Go projects. It walks the AST to compute cyclomatic complexity, merges it with test coverage data from go test -cover, and produces a CRAP score per function.

Inspired by cargo-crap for Rust.

docs.title: What It Does
docs.text:
Point it at a directory - it scans all Go modules, computes complexity, reads coverage, and outputs a ranked table of functions by CRAP score:

go-crap scan

docs.title: CRAP Score
docs.text:
The CRAP score measures how expensive a function is to test. It combines two factors:

  • Cyclomatic complexity (CC) - how many independent paths through the function
  • Test coverage - how much of the function is exercised by tests

A high CRAP score means the function is complex and poorly tested - it's a prime candidate for refactoring or more tests.

→ CRAP Score Formula

docs.title: Missing Coverage Policy
docs.text:
When a function has no coverage data, go-crap can handle it three ways:

  • pessimistic (default) - assume 0% coverage, giving the maximum CRAP score
  • optimistic - assume 100% coverage, giving the minimum CRAP score
  • skip - exclude the function from results entirely

→ Missing Coverage Policy

docs.title: Output Formats
docs.text: Format Flag Use case table default Human-readable terminal output with status symbols json --format json Machine-readable output for CI pipelines github --format github GitHub Actions workflow annotations

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Install
go install github.com/padiazg/go-crap@latest

# Scan a project
go-crap scan

# Show only the 10 worst offenders
go-crap scan --top 10

# Fail CI if any function exceeds threshold
go-crap scan --fail-above --threshold 30

→ Full Quick Start

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
go install github.com/padiazg/go-crap@latest

→ Installation Guide

docs.title: Changelog
docs.text:
All notable changes to go-crap will be documented in this file.

The format is based on Keep a Changelog, and this project adheres to Semantic Versioning.

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Initial release of go-crap
  • scan command - analyze Go modules and calculate CRAP scores
  • AST-based cyclomatic complexity via internal/complexity (adapted from gocyclo, BSD-3-Clause)
  • Coverage profiling via internal/coverage (adapted from test-finder, MIT)
  • Double-index merge - joins coverage and complexity by (filepath, funcname) without CWD path resolution issues
  • CRAP formula - CC^2 x (1 - coverage/100)^3 + CC
  • Missing coverage policy - pessimistic (default), optimistic, skip
  • Output formatters:
  • table - human-readable with status symbols (checkmark, A, x) and coverage bars
  • json - structured output with schema URL
  • github - GitHub Actions workflow annotation format
  • Filtering - --top N, --min score, --exclude glob
  • CI integration - --fail-above exits with code 1, --format github produces workflow annotations
  • GitHub Actions CI/CD workflow (tests + CRAP check)
  • GitHub Pages documentation deployment

docs.title: scan
docs.text: The scan command is the main entry point. It analyzes Go modules, computes CRAP scores, and outputs the results.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
go-crap scan [path] [flags]

Arguments:

Argument Description Default path Directory or package pattern to scan . (current directory)

docs.title: Flags
docs.text: Flag Short Description Default --threshold -t Score above which a function is marked as problematic 30.0 --fail-above Exit with code 1 if any function exceeds the threshold false --format -f Output format: table, json, or github table --top Show only the N worst offenders (0 = all) 0 --min Hide entries below this score 0 --missing Policy for functions without coverage: pessimistic, optimistic, or skip pessimistic --exclude Exclude files matching this glob (repeatable) none

docs.title: Scan all packages
docs.text: go-crap scan

docs.title: Scan a project somewhere else
docs.text: go-crap scan ~/go/src/github.com/padiazg/go-crap

docs.title: Show only the top 20 worst offenders
docs.text: go-crap scan --top 20

docs.title: CI integration - fail on high CRAP scores
docs.text: go-crap scan --fail-above --threshold 30 --format github

docs.title: Filter by minimum score
docs.text: go-crap scan --min 10

docs.title: Exclude generated or test files
docs.text: go-crap scan --exclude '*_test.go' --exclude '*/testdata/*'

docs.title: Machine-readable JSON output
docs.text: go-crap scan --format json > report.json

docs.title: Concepts
docs.text: go-crap computes the CRAP score by combining two independent analyses and merging them:

docs.title: Pipeline Overview
docs.text:
go-crap scan
│
├── coverage.Scan()     - discover Go modules, run go test -cover
│       |
|       └── coverage data per (file, func)
|
├── complexity.Analyze() - walk AST, compute cyclomatic complexity
|       |
|       └── complexity data per (file, func)
|
├── merge.Merge()       - join by (filepath, funcname)
|       |
|       └── merged entries with CC + Coverage
|
├── score.Score()       - apply CRAP formula + missing policy
|       |
|       └── CRAP entries
|
└── report.Format()     - table / json / github
        |
        └── output

docs.title: The Modules
docs.text:
Each module is independently testable:

Module Purpose internal/complexity AST walking to compute cyclomatic complexity (adapted from gocyclo, BSD-3-Clause) internal/coverage Module discovery + go test -cover profiling (adapted from test-finder, MIT) internal/merge Join coverage and complexity by (filepath, funcname) using a double index internal/score CRAP formula + missing coverage policy internal/report Output formatters (table, JSON, GitHub annotations)

docs.title: Important: Path Matching
docs.text: Coverage and complexity produce paths in different formats. The merge layer uses a double index to match them - never canonicalize relative paths against CWD. See internal/merge/index.go and the test TestMerge_RelativePathsNotResolvedAgainstCWD.

docs.title: CRAP Score
docs.text: CRAP stands for Cyclomatic Readability And Predictability. It measures how expensive a function is to test.

docs.title: The Formula
docs.text:
\[ CRAP\left(CC, coverage\right) = CC^2 * \left(1 - \frac{coverage}{100} \right)^3 + CC \]

Where:

  • \(CC\) - cyclomatic complexity (number of independent paths)
  • \(coverage\) - test coverage as a percentage (0-100)

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text: CRAP Range Meaning 0 - 10 Well-tested, simple function 10 - 30 Moderate complexity, should be tested 30 - 50 High CRAP - refactoring or more tests needed 50+ Critical - likely hard to test, complex logic

docs.title: Cyclomatic Complexity
docs.text:
Computed by walking the AST and counting decision points:

  • if, else if, for, case, &&, ||, ?

Each decision point adds 1 to the base complexity of 1.

docs.title: Coverage
docs.text: Obtained by running go test -coverprofile=... and reading the coverage profile. Coverage is expressed as a percentage of lines executed within each function.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
A function with CC=5 and 75% coverage:

\[ \begin{align} CRAP &= 5^2 * (1 - 0.75)^3 + 5 \\ &= 25 * 0.015625 + 5 \\ &= 0.39 + 5 \\ &= 5.39 \\ \end{align} \]

The same function with 0% coverage:

\[ \begin{align} CRAP &= 5^2 * (1 - 0)^3 + 5 \\ &= 25 * 1 + 5 \\ &= 30.0 \\ \end{align} \]

As coverage drops, the CRAP score rises - poorly tested complex functions score highest.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text: The original CRAP score was introduced by Adam Tornelius. go-crap uses the same formula as the cargo-crap Rust implementation.

docs.title: Missing Coverage Policy
docs.text: When go-crap finds a function that has no coverage data (because it has no tests, or the test run didn't cover it), it needs a strategy. The --missing flag controls this behavior.

docs.title: Options
docs.text: Policy Flag Effect pessimistic --missing pessimistic (default) Assume 0% coverage x maximum CRAP score optimistic --missing optimistic Assume 100% coverage x minimum CRAP score skip --missing skip Exclude the function from results

docs.title: Pessimistic (default)
docs.text:
Assumes functions without coverage have 0% test coverage. This gives the highest possible CRAP score and surfaces them as top issues.

go-crap scan --missing pessimistic

A function with CC=8 and 0% coverage:

\[ CRAP = 8^2 x (1 - 0)^3 + 8 = 64 + 8 = 72.00 \]

docs.title: Optimistic
docs.text:
Assumes functions without coverage have 100% coverage. This gives the minimum possible CRAP score - useful for surveys where you want to see the "best case" picture.

go-crap scan --missing optimistic

A function with CC=8 and 100% assumed coverage:

\[ CRAP = 8^2 * \left(1 - 1\right)^3 + 8 = 0 + 8 = 8.00 \]

docs.title: Skip
docs.text:
Excludes functions without coverage from the output entirely. Useful for ignoring untested functions and focusing only on those that have coverage data.

go-crap scan --missing skip

Functions without coverage appear in the output with Skipped: true and their CRAP score equals their complexity.

docs.title: When to Use Each
docs.text:
• pessimistic - CI checks, code reviews, finding the worst issues first
  • optimistic - rough estimation, best-case scenario analysis
  • skip - auditing covered code only, ignoring untested functions

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: • Go 1.22 or later

docs.title: Install via go install
docs.text:
go install github.com/padiazg/go-crap@latest

The binary is placed in $GOPATH/bin (or $GOBIN if set). Make sure that directory is in your PATH.

docs.title: Build from Source
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/padiazg/go-crap.git
cd go-crap
go build -o go-crap .

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
go-crap scan --help

This prints the command help text, confirming the binary is working.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: This guide walks through your first CRAP score scan in under two minutes.

docs.title: 1. Install go-crap
docs.text: go install github.com/padiazg/go-crap@latest

docs.title: 2. Run a Scan
docs.text:
Point go-crap scan at a directory or package pattern:

go-crap scan

Example output:

┌───┬────────┬────┬───────────────────┬──────────┬─────────────────────────────────────┐
│   │ CRAP   │ CC │ COVERAGE          │ FUNCTION │ LOCATION                            |
├───┼────────┼────┼───────────────────┼──────────┼─────────────────────────────────────┤
| ✗ | 156.25 | 12 | ░░░░░░░░░░        | Analyze  │ internal/complexity/complexity.go:5 │
| ▲ │  42.50 |  5 │ ████░░░░░░        │ Scan     │ internal/coverage/coverage.go:28    │
| ✓ │   4.00 │  2 │ ██████████ 100.0% │ Execute  │ cmd/root.go:170                     │
└───┴────────┴────┴───────────────────┴──────────┴─────────────────────────────────────┘

1/3 function(s) exceed threshold CRAP 30.

Columns:

  • Status - checkmark below 50% threshold, A between 50-100%, x above threshold
  • CRAP - the computed CRAP score
  • CC - cyclomatic complexity
  • Coverage - test coverage as a percentage with a visual bar
  • Function - function name
  • Location - file path and line number

docs.title: Show only the worst offenders
docs.text: go-crap scan --top 10

docs.title: Hide entries below a minimum score
docs.text: go-crap scan --min 10

docs.title: Exclude files matching a glob
docs.text: go-crap scan --exclude '*/testdata/*' --exclude '*_test.go'

docs.title: 4. Fail CI on High Scores
docs.text:
go-crap scan --fail-above --threshold 30

Exits with code 1 if any function's CRAP score exceeds 30.

docs.title: JSON
docs.text:
go-crap scan --format json

Output:

{
  "$schema": "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/padiazg/go-crap/main/schemas/report-v1.json",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "entries": [
    {
      "file": "internal/complexity/complexity.go",
      "package": "github.com/padiazg/go-crap/internal/complexity",
      "function": "Analyze",
      "line": 45,
      "cyclomatic": 12,
      "coverage": 0.0,
      "crap": 156.25
    }
  ]
}

docs.title: GitHub Actions annotations
docs.text:
go-crap scan --format github

Emits ::warning annotations that GitHub Actions renders as PR comments.

docs.title: 6. Control Missing Coverage Policy
docs.text:
When a function has no coverage data, decide how to handle it:

# Assume 0% coverage (default) - worst case
go-crap scan --missing pessimistic

# Assume 100% coverage - best case
go-crap scan --missing optimistic

# Skip functions with no coverage
go-crap scan --missing skip

docs.title: The CRAP Score
docs.text:
• cargo-crap - Rust implementation that inspired go-crap
  • gocyclo - Cyclomatic complexity tool for Go (BSD-3-Clause), adapted for internal/complexity
  • test-finder - Test discovery tool (MIT), adapted for internal/coverage

docs.title: Metrics
docs.text:
• Cyclomatic Complexity - software complexity metric by Thomas J. McCabe Sr.
  • CRAP Score - Cyclomatic Readability and Predictability score
